jokes.text: And, um, if my brother Stan is watching, I'll see you Monday morning at 11:00, Stan. I just saved 95 cents on a phone call there.
jokes.setup: Larry addresses his brother watching at home
jokes.punchline: Jokes about saving money on a phone call by using TV time
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry Sanders meta-humor - using his show for personal business while acknowledging the cheapness of it. Well-structured and character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: And, um, if my brother Stan is watching, I'll see you Monday morning at 11:00, Stan. I just saved 95 cents on a phone call there.

jokes.text: Ahoy, captain Sanders. The u.s.s. Hilarity is now safely in her berth. You know, has anyone ever told you you should start wearing a sailor's cap?
jokes.setup: Hank uses elaborate nautical metaphor for the show ending
jokes.punchline: The overly elaborate metaphor followed by the sailor's cap suggestion
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank character comedy - his tendency toward theatrical overstatement and inappropriate suggestions. The nautical metaphor is wonderfully excessive.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Ahoy, captain Sanders. The u.s.s. Hilarity is now safely in her berth. You know, has anyone ever told you you should start wearing a sailor's cap?

jokes.text: Yeah. The late rock hudson.
jokes.setup: Hank asks if anyone told Larry to wear a sailor's cap
jokes.punchline: Larry deadpans that the late Rock Hudson suggested it
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark humor referencing Rock Hudson's sexuality and death. Perfect economy and deadpan delivery. The awkwardness that follows sells it.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: Yeah. The late rock hudson.

jokes.text: Oh, nothing. I'm just practicing my Chinese.
jokes.setup: Larry asks what Hank's 'Aw' means
jokes.punchline: Hank claims he was practicing Chinese
jokes.explanation: Typical Hank deflection with absurd excuse. The randomness works for his character but isn't particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Oh, nothing. I'm just practicing my Chinese.

jokes.text: Because you know what? I'm out of envelopes.
jokes.setup: Hank suggests sending Stan a check instead of hosting him
jokes.punchline: Larry's absurdly mundane excuse for not sending money
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry cheapness disguised as a practical problem. The banality of the excuse makes it funnier than a direct admission of stinginess.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: Because you know what? I'm out of envelopes.

jokes.text: The pecker hasn't even shown up yet.
jokes.setup: Discussion about Stan bringing his dog
jokes.punchline: Artie's crude nickname for the dog
jokes.explanation: Pure Artie - crude and direct. Works for character consistency but isn't particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: The pecker hasn't even shown up yet.

jokes.text: Oh, my God. I forgot Stan was even coming.
jokes.setup: Larry has been discussing Stan's visit throughout the scene
jokes.punchline: Claims to have forgotten despite just talking about him
jokes.explanation: Shows Larry's self-absorption and tendency to panic. Mildly amusing character beat but not particularly strong.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: Oh, my God. I forgot Stan was even coming.

jokes.text: Now, I shouldn't have tipped that guy, right? No, I... I completely took care of that. You don't have to worry.
jokes.setup: Stan arrives and mentions tipping the limo driver
jokes.punchline: Larry's awkward reassurance that reveals he didn't tip at all
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry cheapness moment. The phrasing suggests he tipped when he clearly didn't, creating awkward comedy through his deception.

dialogue.speaker: Stan
dialogue.line: Now, I shouldn't have tipped that guy, right?

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: No, I... I completely took care of that. You don't have to worry.

jokes.text: Great. Now, is this an original? Don't touch that. Just come on in.
jokes.setup: Stan admires something in Larry's house
jokes.punchline: Larry's immediate protective response
jokes.explanation: Shows Larry's materialism and distrust even of family. Quick character beat that establishes their dynamic.

dialogue.speaker: Stan
dialogue.line: Great. Now, is this an original?

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: Don't touch that. Just come on in.

jokes.text: With duchovny and Seinfeld? Oh, well, those guys, they're working, but I thought we'd play, you know, just together.
jokes.setup: Larry suggests playing basketball
jokes.punchline: Stan assumes it's with celebrities; Larry has to clarify it's just them
jokes.explanation: Great character comedy showing Stan's assumptions about Larry's lifestyle versus reality. The deflation is perfectly awkward.

dialogue.speaker: Stan
dialogue.line: With duchovny and Seinfeld?

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: Oh, well, those guys, they're working, but I thought we'd play, you know, just together.

jokes.text: Just happy to be out of rehab. I'm kidding.
jokes.setup: Asked how he feels about being the newest star of late-night TV
jokes.punchline: Claims to be happy to be out of rehab, then immediately clarifies he's kidding
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection joke about showbiz stereotypes. The immediate clarification adds to the humor by showing his nervousness about the joke.

dialogue.speaker: Jon Stewart
dialogue.line: Just happy to be out of rehab. I'm kidding.

jokes.text: Yes. I called the tracking number, and the people at omaha said it was delivered on Saturday. Saturday? Mm-hmm. Well, Jesus, that's $200 worth of meat.
jokes.setup: Discussion about meat sent to Jon Stewart
jokes.punchline: Hank's obsession with the expensive meat and lack of thank you note
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank behavior - the elaborate gift followed by obsessing over acknowledgment. The meat tracking detail is wonderfully specific.

dialogue.speaker: Assistant
dialogue.line: Yes. I called the tracking number, and the people at omaha said it was delivered on Saturday.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Saturday? Mm-hmm.

dialogue.speaker: Assistant
dialogue.line: Well, Jesus, that's $200 worth of meat.

jokes.text: Well, Jesus, that's $200 worth of meat. I mean... no 'thank you' note. I'm fucked.
jokes.setup: Learning Jon received the expensive meat delivery
jokes.punchline: Hank's dramatic overreaction to lack of thank-you note
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank neurosis - treating etiquette as life-or-death. The escalation from gift to existential crisis is beautifully absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Hank Kingsley
dialogue.line: Well, Jesus, that's $200 worth of meat. I mean... no 'thank you' note. I'm fucked.

jokes.text: Actually, it was $100 because you went with the strip steaks instead of the filets.
jokes.setup: Hank complains about $200 worth of unacknowledged meat
jokes.punchline: Assistant corrects him with specific knowledge of his cheapness
jokes.explanation: Excellent deflation of Hank's complaint. The assistant's specific knowledge of his cost-cutting measures makes it funnier and more embarrassing.

dialogue.speaker: Assistant
dialogue.line: Actually, it was $100 because you went with the strip steaks instead of the filets.

jokes.text: Great news, Hank. You're in tonight's sketch. I am? Mm-hmm. All right. That's great. Uh, what am I doing? You're playing hitler.
jokes.setup: Hank is excited about being in a sketch
jokes.punchline: The reveal that he's playing Hitler
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup and escalation. Hank's enthusiasm makes the Hitler reveal more jarring and uncomfortable, which fits the show's cringe aesthetic.

dialogue.speaker: Writer
dialogue.line: Great news, Hank. You're in tonight's sketch.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I am?

dialogue.speaker: Writer
dialogue.line: Mm-hmm.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: All right. That's great. Uh, what am I doing?

dialogue.speaker: Writer
dialogue.line: You're playing hitler.

jokes.text: The hitler? Yeah, one in the same.
jokes.setup: Hank seeks clarification about playing Hitler
jokes.punchline: Writer confirms it's THE Hitler with casual 'one and the same'
jokes.explanation: The casual confirmation and Hank's need for clarification adds to the uncomfortable humor. The writer's blasé attitude contrasts with the gravity.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: The hitler?

dialogue.speaker: Writer
dialogue.line: Yeah, one in the same.

jokes.text: I'm fucked. Comedy central. Oh, come on. I'll have to wear something tonight that'll make me look young and fresh.
jokes.setup: Hank learns Jon is considering a young comedian for sidekick
jokes.punchline: His plan to compete by dressing young and fresh
jokes.explanation: Classic Hank delusion about being able to appear young through wardrobe. Shows his vanity and desperation perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I'm fucked. Comedy central. Oh, come on. I'll have to wear something tonight that'll make me look young and fresh.

jokes.text: That's not young and fresh. This is not young. This... this is old. This is show business.
jokes.setup: Hank examining his appearance
jokes.punchline: His brutal self-assessment ending with 'this is show business'
jokes.explanation: Great Hank moment of self-awareness followed by industry cynicism. The progression from specific to general is well-crafted.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: That's not young and fresh. This is not young. This... this is old. This is show business.

jokes.text: Doesn't this smell like urine? Let's not put a name to it. Let me just get you the spray.
jokes.setup: Hank asks assistant to smell something
jokes.punchline: Assistant's diplomatic refusal to identify the smell
jokes.explanation: 'Let's not put a name to it' is a beautifully diplomatic way to handle Hank's gross request. Perfect professional boundary-setting humor.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Doesn't this smell like urine?

dialogue.speaker: Assistant
dialogue.line: Let's not put a name to it. Let me just get you the spray.

jokes.text: Why is it always an asian woman? 2 birds with one stone. What does that mean?
jokes.setup: Casting call for sketch needs asian woman
jokes.punchline: Writer's casual 'two birds with one stone' about diversity casting
jokes.explanation: Sharp satire of Hollywood diversity tokenism. The casual cruelty of the 'two birds' comment followed by confused response is perfectly uncomfortable.

dialogue.speaker: Writer
dialogue.line: Why is it always an asian woman?

dialogue.speaker: Writer
dialogue.line: 2 birds with one stone.

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: What does that mean?

jokes.text: Now, there are jokes, and then there is piss all over my shoes. These are 2... entirely different things.
jokes.setup: Hank addresses the room about someone urinating on his shoes
jokes.punchline: His philosophical distinction between jokes and urine
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Hank speechifying about a gross situation. The formal tone applied to urine is perfect character comedy. Highly quotable and memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Now, there are jokes, and then there is piss all over my shoes. These are 2... entirely different things.

jokes.text: 'cause I had asparagus last night.
jokes.setup: Hank asks if Phil pissed on his shoes
jokes.punchline: Phil's overly specific denial involving asparagus
jokes.explanation: Gross and specific detail that makes the denial funnier. The logic of 'you'd know if it was me because asparagus' is perfectly absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: 'cause I had asparagus last night.

jokes.text: I think that was probably my dog. He's got a bladder infection from, I think, rough sex.
jokes.setup: Jon takes responsibility for the urine
jokes.punchline: Attributes dog's bladder infection to rough sex
jokes.explanation: Unnecessarily specific and inappropriate explanation that makes the apology worse. The TMI aspect fits the show's cringe aesthetic.

dialogue.speaker: Jon Stewart
dialogue.line: I think that was probably my dog. He's got a bladder infection from, I think, rough sex.

jokes.text: What can we do, just tie their assholes in a knot? I think that's balloon animals.
jokes.setup: Hank philosophizes about dogs holding it in all day
jokes.punchline: Phil's dry observation that asshole-tying is balloon animals
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist humor. Phil's deadpan correction of Hank's crude suggestion with balloon animal reference is perfectly unexpected and quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: What can we do, just tie their assholes in a knot?

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: I think that's balloon animals.

jokes.text: They're loyal. They stick by their masters. Unless, of course, their masters abandon them. And then they have to find a new master. But when they find their new master, they're loyal forever. Assuming, of course, their new master wants them.
jokes.setup: Hank talks about dog loyalty
jokes.punchline: Obvious metaphor about his own job insecurity with Jon as new host
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank - unable to be subtle about his anxiety. The extended metaphor is painfully obvious and desperate, which makes it funny.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: They're loyal. They stick by their masters. Unless, of course, their masters abandon them. And then they have to find a new master. But when they find their new master, they're loyal forever. Assuming, of course, their new master wants them.

jokes.text: Well, we think they might be a little too... urban. Urban? Well, I can call my good friend lenny kravitz. He's only half-urban.
jokes.setup: Network executives worry Wu-Tang Clan is too 'urban'
jokes.punchline: Artie's sarcastic offer of Lenny Kravitz as 'half-urban'
jokes.explanation: Sharp satire of network racism disguised as programming concerns. Artie's 'half-urban' comment perfectly skewers their coded language.

dialogue.speaker: Kenny
dialogue.line: Well, we think they might be a little too... urban.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Urban? Well, I can call my good friend lenny kravitz. He's only half-urban.

jokes.text: we don't want this to turn into a fucking freak show like they've got over there at conan.
jokes.setup: Network discussing Jon Stewart's direction
jokes.punchline: Calling Conan's show a 'freak show'
jokes.explanation: Good industry insider joke about late-night competition. The casual dismissal of Conan fits the network executive character.

dialogue.speaker: Executive
dialogue.line: we don't want this to turn into a fucking freak show like they've got over there at conan.

jokes.text: Well, I don't wanna piss out your comedy campfire here, but we have a minor problem.
jokes.setup: Artie interrupting comedy discussion
jokes.punchline: His mixed metaphor of 'pissing out a campfire'
jokes.explanation: Classic Artie colorful language. The mixed metaphor works because it's crude but oddly poetic, fitting his character perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Well, I don't wanna piss out your comedy campfire here, but we have a minor problem.

jokes.text: Jon, have you ever started dating a woman, treated her very gently, took her to fine restaurants, opened doors for her, charmed her parents? And you gave her some hugs, but you didn't even attempt to kiss her for a long time? Then, one day, in your car, in broad daylight, suddenly, she jumps on you and starts giving you a blow job. Right. The network wants you to bump Wu-tang clan.
jokes.setup: Artie's elaborate sexual metaphor about courtship
jokes.punchline: Connects it to network wanting to bump Wu-Tang Clan
jokes.explanation: Masterful misdirection from Artie. The elaborate sexual metaphor seems like it's going somewhere else entirely, making the punchline perfect. Pure Artie character work.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Jon, have you ever started dating a woman, treated her very gently, took her to fine restaurants, opened doors for her, charmed her parents? And you gave her some hugs, but you didn't even attempt to kiss her for a long time? Then, one day, in your car, in broad daylight, suddenly, she jumps on you and starts giving you a blow job. Right. The network wants you to bump Wu-tang clan.

jokes.text: So I'm not getting a blow job.
jokes.setup: Jon processes Artie's metaphor
jokes.punchline: Reduces it to the key disappointment
jokes.explanation: Perfect comedic reduction of Artie's elaborate metaphor to its essence. Shows Jon's dry wit and understanding of what really matters.

dialogue.speaker: Jon Stewart
dialogue.line: So I'm not getting a blow job.

jokes.text: they want you to build up a broad base of fan support before you start scaring the shit out of the midwest.
jokes.setup: Explaining network's position on Wu-Tang
jokes.punchline: Crude but accurate assessment of middle America's fears
jokes.explanation: Artie's blunt assessment of network fears and demographics. The phrase 'scaring the shit out of the midwest' is perfectly crude and accurate.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: they want you to build up a broad base of fan support before you start scaring the shit out of the midwest.

jokes.text: the home-schooled heartthrobs known as hanson, the 1995 country music's best new artist 'achy breaky' Billy Ray cyrus, and the reunited, reinvigorated, rehabilitated, three dog night.
jokes.setup: Artie offering replacement acts for Wu-Tang
jokes.punchline: The increasingly pathetic options, culminating in has-been Three Dog Night
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing the network's terrible taste, with nice alliteration in 'reunited, reinvigorated, rehabilitated.'

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: the home-schooled heartthrobs known as hanson, the 1995 country music's best new artist 'achy breaky' Billy Ray cyrus, and the reunited, reinvigorated, rehabilitated, three dog night.

jokes.text: I have a policy... never bump a band that's packin' heat.
jokes.setup: Jon considering whether to bump Wu-Tang Clan
jokes.punchline: His policy about not bumping armed bands
jokes.explanation: Great dry humor about practical showbiz concerns. The matter-of-fact delivery of this 'policy' makes it funnier than if played for shock.

dialogue.speaker: Jon Stewart
dialogue.line: I have a policy... never bump a band that's packin' heat.

jokes.text: Oh, my God, they look like carjackers.
jokes.setup: Hank looking at Wu-Tang Clan's CD
jokes.punchline: His racist assessment of their appearance
jokes.explanation: Pure character comedy showing Hank's casual racism, but relies on stereotype for the joke. Works for character but isn't particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Oh, my God, they look like carjackers.

jokes.text: I gotta tell you, my absolute favorite song is 'shame on a nigga,' you know? Shame on a nigga? Shame on a nigga? No? It's a phat song, you know.
jokes.setup: Hank trying to connect with Wu-Tang Clan
jokes.punchline: His awkward attempt to use their lyrics and slang
jokes.explanation: Classic Hank trying too hard to be cool and failing spectacularly. The cringe factor works but the humor is somewhat predictable.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I gotta tell you, my absolute favorite song is 'shame on a nigga,' you know? Shame on a nigga? Shame on a nigga? No? It's a phat song, you know.

jokes.text: Where... where is she? Yo, yo, he just got out of jail. You know what I mean? He's just holding down at home for a while, you know? Oh, man, gee, I know that dig, you know.
jokes.setup: Hank asks about 'dirty old bitch' (meaning ODB)
jokes.punchline: His attempt to relate to someone being in jail
jokes.explanation: Hank's misguided attempt to relate to street credibility through his minor legal troubles. The disconnect is painful and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Where... where is she?

dialogue.speaker: Wu-Tang member
dialogue.line: Yo, yo, he just got out of jail. You know what I mean? He's just holding down at home for a while, you know?

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Oh, man, gee, I know that dig, you know.

jokes.text: I got parking tickets coming right out of my ass. You know? Fuckers, cops, you know? Fuckers. You know?
jokes.setup: Hank trying to relate to Wu-Tang's legal troubles
jokes.punchline: His pathetic attempt to sound street by complaining about parking tickets
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank delusion - thinking parking tickets give him street cred. The repeated 'fuckers' shows him trying too hard to sound tough.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I got parking tickets coming right out of my ass. You know? Fuckers, cops, you know? Fuckers. You know?

jokes.text: I just... on behalf of all my people, I'd like to apologize.
jokes.setup: Jon witnessing Hank's embarrassing interaction
jokes.punchline: Apologizing 'on behalf of all my people' (white people/Jews)
jokes.explanation: Smart self-aware humor about racial embarrassment. Jon's diplomatic way of distancing himself from Hank while acknowledging shared identity.

dialogue.speaker: Jon Stewart
dialogue.line: I just... on behalf of all my people, I'd like to apologize.

jokes.text: Yo, these cats is Bunk.
jokes.setup: Wu-Tang's assessment after meeting Hank and Jon
jokes.punchline: Their blunt dismissal
jokes.explanation: Perfect capper to the awkward interaction. Their honest assessment of the show people they just met is refreshingly direct.

dialogue.speaker: Wu-Tang member
dialogue.line: Yo, these cats is Bunk.

jokes.text: Until 10 seconds ago, I was blissfully unaware that such a hitler sketch existed.
jokes.setup: Network executives confronting them about Hitler sketch
jokes.punchline: Artie's claim of ignorance despite being the producer
jokes.explanation: Classic Artie deflection - claiming ignorance of something he should know about. The 'blissfully unaware' phrasing is perfectly theatrical.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Until 10 seconds ago, I was blissfully unaware that such a hitler sketch existed.

jokes.text: Yeah, well, that's why carson's an idiot. 'Cause if he'd just done a pay per view at $39 a pop, he would've made $800 million one night.
jokes.setup: Discussion about Carson's last show getting 20 million viewers
jokes.punchline: Stan's scheme to monetize Carson's farewell via pay-per-view
jokes.explanation: Perfect Stan character work - seeing everything through a money-making lens. Calling Carson an 'idiot' for not monetizing his farewell is pure Stan.

dialogue.speaker: Stan
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, that's why carson's an idiot. 'Cause if he'd just done a pay per view at $39 a pop, he would've made $800 million one night.

jokes.text: You leave me out there with my pants around my ankles, vaseline on my ass, I don't know what the fuck happened.
jokes.setup: Artie complaining about being blindsided by Jon's sketch
jokes.punchline: His graphic metaphor for being screwed over
jokes.explanation: Classic Artie colorful language to describe being betrayed. The graphic sexual metaphor is perfectly crude and specific to his character.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: You leave me out there with my pants around my ankles, vaseline on my ass, I don't know what the fuck happened.

jokes.text: So is Jesus, and we all know where he ended up. Yeah, on the wb.
jokes.setup: Jon says he's Jewish like Jesus when defending Hitler sketch
jokes.punchline: Artie sets up Jesus's crucifixion, Jon deflects to WB network
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misdirection. Sets up for obvious crucifixion reference but deflects to industry joke about WB network. Perfect example of show's smart writing.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: So is Jesus, and we all know where he ended up.

dialogue.speaker: Jon Stewart
dialogue.line: Yeah, on the wb.

jokes.text: I'm actually a very masculine alien trapped in mickey rooney's body. Isn't that sad?
jokes.setup: Following up on Kristin Johnson's alien character description
jokes.punchline: Jon's version involving Mickey Rooney
jokes.explanation: Decent callback to the alien concept but with Mickey Rooney twist. Works as interview banter but not particularly strong.

dialogue.speaker: Jon Stewart
dialogue.line: I'm actually a very masculine alien trapped in mickey rooney's body. Isn't that sad?

jokes.text: Heil now!
jokes.setup: Hank enters as Hitler character for game show sketch
jokes.punchline: Pun combining 'Heil' with game show greeting
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective pun for the sketch premise. The combination of Nazi greeting with game show enthusiasm is appropriately uncomfortable.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Heil now!

jokes.text: No, I'm sorry. The correct response is, 'what were the Jews?'
jokes.setup: Game show question about what the Titanic hit
jokes.punchline: Hitler character's antisemitic 'correction'
jokes.explanation: The joke that gets the sketch pulled. While it fits the premise, it's more shocking than clever, relying on antisemitism for impact rather than wit.

dialogue.speaker: Hank as Adolf Hankler
dialogue.line: No, I'm sorry. The correct response is, 'what were the Jews?'

jokes.text: This is really inappropriate. This is offensive. I'm sorry, I can't follow this. I can't go on after this. Didn't you do nazi stuff on Seinfeld? Yeah, that's an entirely different situation.
jokes.setup: Jason Alexander refusing to go on after Hitler sketch
jokes.punchline: Reminder that he did Nazi material on Seinfeld, his dismissive response
jokes.explanation: Smart meta-humor about Alexander's own Nazi-adjacent comedy on Seinfeld. His dismissive 'entirely different situation' perfectly captures celebrity hypocrisy.

dialogue.speaker: Jason Alexander
dialogue.line: This is really inappropriate. This is offensive. I'm sorry, I can't follow this. I can't go on after this.
Didn't you do nazi stuff on Seinfeld?

dialogue.speaker: Jason Alexander
dialogue.line: Yeah, that's an entirely different situation.

jokes.text: He's 'hitlarious.'
jokes.setup: Executive describing Jon Stewart
jokes.punchline: Pun combining Hitler and hilarious
jokes.explanation: Lazy pun but works for showing executive's tone-deafness. The fact that he thinks this is clever makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Network Executive
dialogue.line: He's 'hitlarious.'

jokes.text: I'm sorry for coming out here and fucking everything up for you, but some of us have to work for a living. We don't go onstage and tell jokes and cash checks and fuck actresses.
jokes.setup: Stan's resentment building throughout their argument
jokes.punchline: His bitter summary of Larry's privileged life
jokes.explanation: Perfect Stan bitterness. The progression from apology to attack, ending with the celebrity lifestyle summary, is well-crafted family resentment.

dialogue.speaker: Stan
dialogue.line: I'm sorry for coming out here and fucking everything up for you, but some of us have to work for a living. We don't go onstage and tell jokes and cash checks and fuck actresses.

jokes.text: You are selfish. You're selish now. You were selfish when you were 10. It's always you and your career and your show and your therapist, and everyone else is second, me and mom and everybody.
jokes.setup: Stan's accumulated resentment toward Larry
jokes.punchline: Including 'your therapist' in the list of Larry's priorities
jokes.explanation: Good character work showing Stan's long-term resentment. The therapist inclusion is a nice touch showing his disdain for Larry's Hollywood lifestyle.

dialogue.speaker: Stan
dialogue.line: You are selfish. You're selish now. You were selfish when you were 10. It's always you and your career and your show and your therapist, and everyone else is second, me and mom and everybody.

jokes.text: All right, just tell me the names of my kids. Answer me that.
jokes.setup: Stan proving Larry's selfishness
jokes.punchline: The ultimate test - Larry doesn't know his nephews' names
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation and proof of Stan's point. The challenge is simple but devastating, and Larry's silence confirms everything Stan said.

dialogue.speaker: Stan
dialogue.line: All right, just tell me the names of my kids. Answer me that.

jokes.text: I believe tonight will be a best of Larry.
jokes.setup: Complete show cancellation due to Hitler sketch disaster
jokes.punchline: Artie's matter-of-fact delivery of the ultimate fallback programming
jokes.explanation: Good understated delivery of total failure - the show falls apart so completely they resort to reruns.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: I believe tonight will be a best of Larry.

jokes.text: There's only 2. One's a girl.
jokes.setup: Stan revealing Larry can't name his kids
jokes.punchline: Making it even easier by giving Larry hints
jokes.explanation: Perfect capper to the kids' names challenge. The simplicity of the hint makes Larry's failure even more damning and pathetic.

dialogue.speaker: Stan
dialogue.line: There's only 2. One's a girl.
